text: The surgeon introduces a liver retractor to elevate the left lobe of the liver, providing better access to the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissecting adhesions between the stomach and the left lobe of the liver, using laparoscopic instruments to carefully separate these structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the liver, maintaining proximity to avoid injuring the stomach during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon approaches the site of the previous Nissen fundoplication and performs dissection between the fundoplication and the left lobe of the liver, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects between the nissen wrap and the underlying esophagus, navigating a challenging area to separate these structures laparoscopically.

The surgeon uses both blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate the adhesions between the stomach and the left lobe of the liver, with the liver retractor elevating the left lobe for better access.
The surgeon identifies the nascent wrap at the superior aspect, visually distinguishing it from surrounding anatomical structures.
A nasogastric tube is positioned within the esophagus, providing clear identification of the esophageal structure during the dissection process.
During the dissection between the Nissen fundoplication and the esophagus, the surgeon instructs anesthesia to adjust the nasogastric tube, moving it in and out to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions between the diaphragm and the Nissen fundoplication wrap, using both blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate the structures.
The surgeon has created an adequate window through the dissection, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The nasogastric tube is visible within the stomach, positioned to aid in the identification and manipulation of the gastric structures during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a green load stapler to transect the nissen wrap after obtaining an adequate window, with the nasogastric tube visible in the esophagus.
Following the transection of the nascent wrap, the surgeon dissects the angle of His, carefully separating tissue at this anatomical junction.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions from the previous surgery, using instruments to separate the fibrous tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon proceeds with dissection in the posterior region, likely continuing the surgical approach around the stomach area.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions from the previous surgery, using precise techniques to separate the fibrous tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon begins creating a gastric pouch along the lesser curvature of the stomach, carefully manipulating the tissue to form the pouch structure.
The surgeon transects the lesser omentum using a perigastric approach. A blue load stapler is then used to construct a gastric pouch off the lesser curvature, following the standard technique.
The surgeon continues to dissect adhesions located on the posterior aspect of the stomach while progressively forming the gastric pouch.
The surgeon identifies and addresses adhesions from a previous surgery, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon defines the angle of His from the posterior aspect of the gastric pouch, carefully transecting adhesions from previous surgeries.
The surgeon completes the transection of the gastric pouch on the lesser curvature after obtaining an adequate window, ensuring the pouch is fully separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a green load stapler to transect the gastric tissue, accommodating for the increased thickness due to adhesions from a previous surgery.
The surgeon places clips on the gastric pouch for additional security, ensuring stability and preventing movement during the procedure.
The surgeon elevates the transverse colon and mesocolon to locate the ligament of Treitz after creating the gastric pouch, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon exposes the inferior mesenteric vein and the pancreas, revealing these anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon measures approximately forty centimeters distally from the ligaments of Treitz along the small bowel, using a visible bar as a reference point.
The surgeon transects the small bowel using a white load vascular stapler, visibly dividing the bowel with precision.
The surgeon places a clip on the biliopancreatic limb to ensure identification and security of the correct limb during the procedure.
The surgeon runs the small bowel back to the ligament of Treitz, ensuring proper alignment and positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon leaves the biliopancreatic limb in place, ensuring its correct positioning as part of the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon takes a clip to secure the biliopancreatic limb, ensuring proper identification and positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon elevates the alimentary limb, also known as the Roux limb, towards the gastric pouch, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon begins constructing an anastomosis, visually aligning the alimentary limb with the gastric pouch to facilitate the connection.
The surgeon performs a two-layer gastro-jejunal anastomosis using Vicryl sutures, carefully stitching the gastric pouch to the jejunal limb.
The surgeon constructs the posterior layer of the gastro-jejunal anastomosis using two Vicryl sutures, securing the connection between the gastric pouch and the alimentary limb.
After completing the posterior layer of the anastomosis, the surgeon prepares to make a gastrotomy in the gastric pouch, which involves creating an incision in the stomach tissue.
The surgeon creates an anastomosis using a nasogastric tube, connecting the alimentary limb to the gastric pouch.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the alimentary limb, preparing for the next step in the anastomosis construction.
The surgeon uses a Harmonic Scalpel to perform precise tissue dissection, likely in the context of creating or refining an anastomosis.
The surgeon applies a second layer of stitching using Vicryl sutures to reinforce the posterior layer of the anastomosis.
The surgeon is working on the second posterior layer of sutures in the gastric pouch, following the completion of the posterior anastomosis.
The surgeon takes separate stitches at the corners of the anastomosis to ensure secure bites, using precise suturing technique.
The surgeon begins stitching the third layer, continuing the suturing process observed in previous layers.
The surgeon inserts a thirty-two French tube into the small bowel to calibrate the anastomosis, ensuring the correct size and alignment of the surgical connection.
The surgeon completes the third layer of sutures and begins the fourth layer using two ovicral stitches, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon tests the anastomosis by submerging it underwater and insufflating air to visually check for any leaks.
The surgeon measures approximately one hundred centimeters of the alimentary limb to prepare for the creation of the jejunal jejunostomy.
The surgeon places an anchor stitch between the alimentary limb and the biliopancreatic limb, securing the connection between these two sections of the intestine.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy in each limb, visually opening the alimentary and biliopancreatic limbs for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon performs the anastomosis using a 60mm white load stapler, joining the alimentary and biliopancreatic limbs.
The surgeon closes the infratomy using two micro-stitchers, completing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defects using a silk suture, ensuring the defects are securely sealed.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defects in segments, carefully maneuvering to avoid the mesenteric vessels during the suturing process.
The surgeon uses silk suture to completely close the mesenteric defect between the bowel loops and the mesentery, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
After completing the jejuno-jejunostomy, the surgeon performs a final endoscopy to inspect the surgical site.
The surgical team submerges the anastomosis underwater to check for leaks and ensure the patency of the connection.
